hints: Try replacing every variable by $\mathrm{L},$ since all the variables represent some sort of length. Then ignore any constants. This might help you to see how the dimensions of the variables simplify. For example, we could turn $\dfrac{8\pi}{3d}\left(r_1^3 - r_2^3\right)$ into $\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{L}} \left(\mathrm{L}^3 - \mathrm{L}^3\right).$ Then remember that $\mathrm{L^3} - \mathrm{L}^3 = \mathrm{L}^3,$ not zero, since subtraction only means that we are subtracting a volume from another volume, not that the two volumes are necessarily the same as each other. That means we can simplify the expression to $\dfrac{1}{\mathrm{L}} \mathrm{L}^3 = \mathrm{L}^2,$ and this expression is an area.
problem_text:
[{"motor_ref":"4ca6eb25","motor_type":"DragDropCategories","motor_json":"{\"problemText\":\"The lesson for this problem is that you don't necessarily need to know where a mathematical expression comes from or what it's an expression for in order to make some sense of it. Here, we'll use dimensions to figure out what type of thing (a length, an area, or a volume) various expressions represent. All the expressions in this problem are real mathematical expressions that you could come across in science or math problems, but you don't need to know what problems they come from in order to figure out what type of thing these expressions represent!\\n\\n------------------------------------\\n\\nEach of the following expressions is in terms of some variables. All the variables represent a length of some kind. For example, the variable $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}$ might be the side length of a rectangle and the variable $\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}$ might be the radius of a circle. In this problem, the exact interpretation of each variable is not important. What matters is that each variable is some length. ($\\\\pi$ is not a variable. It is a number with no dimensions. The variables are $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{d}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{\\\\ell}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{h}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{r_1}\\\\color{black}, \\\\color{red}{r_2}\\\\color{black}$ and $\\\\color{red}{R}\\\\color{black}.$ We've colored them red to help you recognize which symbols are variables.)\\n\\nClassify each expression according to whether it is for a length, an area, or a volume.\\n\\nAs an example, let's look at $\\\\sqrt{\\\\color{red}{abc}\\\\color{black}(\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black})}.$ First, we look at $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black} + \\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black} + \\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black}.$ This is the sum of three lengths. When we add any number of lengths, we get another length, so $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black} + \\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black} + \\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black}$ is a length. That means $\\\\color{red}{abc}\\\\color{black}(\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black})$ is four lengths all multiplied together, so that quantity has dimensions $\\\\mathrm{L}^4.$ We are looking at $\\\\sqrt{\\\\color{red}{abc}\\\\color{black}(\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black})},$ which is the square root of something with dimensions of $\\\\mathrm{L}^4,$ so its dimensions are $\\\\sqrt{\\\\mathrm{L^4}} = \\\\mathrm{L^2}.$ These are the dimensions of area, so $\\\\sqrt{\\\\color{red}{abc}\\\\color{black}(\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black})}$ is an area. We can say this confidently, even though we don't know what area it is.\\n\\nAs another simple example, $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}^3$ is a volume because if we replace $\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}$ by its dimension $\\\\mathrm{L},$ the expression becomes $\\\\mathrm{L}^3,$ which is the dimension of a volume. Your job is to classify the remaining expressions.\\n\\nOne of the expressions uses $\\\\mathrm{ln}.$ This is the [i]natural logarithm[\\/i]. It is okay if you haven't learned about logarithms and don't know what $\\\\mathrm{ln}$ means. All you need to know is that $\\\\mathrm{ln}$ is not a variable; it is a function. It does not carry any dimensions, so from a dimensions perspective you can ignore the $\\\\mathrm{ln}.$\\n\",\"type\":\"DragDropCategories\",\"parseFormat\":\"draggablesInEachDropArea\",\"outputDisplayFormat\":\"formattedList\",\"options\":{\"categoryNames\":[\"Length\",\"Area\",\"Volume\"],\"draggableContents\":[\"\\\\[\\\\sqrt{\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}^2 + \\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}^2 + \\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black}^2}\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\dfrac{\\\\sqrt{2}}{2} \\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}^2\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\dfrac{8\\\\pi}{3\\\\color{red}{d}\\\\color{black}} \\\\left(\\\\color{red}{r_1}\\\\color{black}^3 - \\\\color{red}{r_2}\\\\color{black}^3\\\\right)\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\pi (\\\\color{red}{R}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black})\\\\sqrt{(\\\\color{red}{R}\\\\color{black}-\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black})^2 + \\\\color{red}{h}\\\\color{black}^2}\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\dfrac{\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}}{2} \\\\left( \\\\dfrac{\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}}{\\\\color{red}{\\\\ell}\\\\color{black}} \\\\sqrt{1 + \\\\left(\\\\dfrac{\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}}{\\\\color{red}{\\\\ell}\\\\color{black}}\\\\right)^2} + \\\\ln \\\\left( \\\\dfrac{\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}}{\\\\color{red}{\\\\ell}\\\\color{black}} + \\\\sqrt{1 + \\\\left(\\\\dfrac{\\\\color{red}{r}\\\\color{black}}{\\\\color{red}{\\\\ell}\\\\color{black}}\\\\right)^2}\\\\right)\\\\right)\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\sqrt{\\\\color{red}{abc}\\\\color{black}(\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{b}\\\\color{black}+\\\\color{red}{c}\\\\color{black})}\\\\]\",\"\\\\[\\\\color{red}{a}\\\\color{black}^3\\\\]\"],\"height\":400,\"numDraggablesInEachRow\":[1,1,1,1]},\"problem_text_fmt\":\"The lesson for this problem is that you don't necessarily need to know where a mathematical expression comes from or what it's an expression for in order to make some sense of it. Here, we'll use dimensions to figure out what type of thing (a length, an area, or a volume) various expressions represent. All the expressions in this problem are real mathematical expressions that you could come across in science or math problems, but you don't need to know what problems they come from in order to figure out what type of thing these expressions represent!
\\n\\n
\\n
\\nEach of the following expressions is in terms of some variables. All the variables represent a length of some kind. For example, the variable might be the side length of a rectangle and the variable might be the radius of a circle. In this problem, the exact interpretation of each variable is not important. What matters is that each variable is some length. (<\\/span> is not a variable. It is a number with no dimensions. The variables are and We've colored them red to help you recognize which symbols are variables.)
\\n
\\nClassify each expression according to whether it is for a length, an area, or a volume.
\\n
\\nAs an example, let's look at First, we look at This is the sum of three lengths. When we add any number of lengths, we get another length, so is a length. That means is four lengths all multiplied together, so that quantity has dimensions We are looking at which is the square root of something with dimensions of so its dimensions are These are the dimensions of area, so is an area. We can say this confidently, even though we don't know what area it is.
\\n
\\nAs another simple example, is a volume because if we replace by its dimension the expression becomes which is the dimension of a volume. Your job is to classify the remaining expressions.
\\n
\\nOne of the expressions uses This is the natural logarithm<\\/i>. It is okay if you haven't learned about logarithms and don't know what means. All you need to know is that is not a variable; it is a function. It does not carry any dimensions, so from a dimensions perspective you can ignore the
\\n\"}"}]
solution_text:
The correct categorization is:
[center][img]http://cdn.artofproblemsolving.com/school/crypt/00268-5aaef100339f0a68f2a364ddc2eb80306679d9b3/files/02_hw_length_area_volume_answers.jpg[/img][/center]
$\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}$ is a $ oxed{	ext{length}}.$ When we square the variables, we get areas. Adding the areas gives another area. Taking a square root gives a length. This is an expression for a distance in three dimensions, like in a three dimensional version of the Pythagorean theorem.
$\sqrt{abc(a+b+c)}$ is an $ oxed{	ext{area}}.$ $(a+b+c)$ is a length. Multiplying that to three lengths, we have dimensions $\mathrm{L}^4$ in the square root. Taking the square root gives dimensions $\mathrm{L}^2,$ an area. This is an expression for the area of a triangle.
$\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} a^2 b$ is a $ oxed{	ext{volume}}.$ $a^2$ is an area and multiplying that by $b$ we get a volume.
$\dfrac{8\pi}{3d}\left(r_1^3 - r_2^3ight)$ is an $ oxed{	ext{area}}.$ Each of $r_1^3$ and $r_2^3$ are volumes. Subtracting them gives another volume. Dividing by $d$ gives an area. This expression was used by biophysicists to estimate the area of the endoplasmic reticulum, a structure inside a cell.
$\pi\left(R+right)\sqrt{\left(R-right)^2 + h^2}$ is an $ oxed{	ext{area}}$. The quantity inside the square root is an area. Taking the square root gives a length, and multiplying that by $R+r$ gives an area. This is an expression for the surface area of a conical frustum - a cone with the top cut off.
$\dfrac{a}{2}\left( \dfrac{r}{\ell} \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{r}{\ell}ight)^2} + \ln\left(\dfrac{r}{\ell} + \sqrt{1+\left(\dfrac{r}{\ell}ight)^2}ight)ight)$ is a $ oxed{	ext{length}}.$ The quantity $\dfrac{r}{\ell}$ is dimensionless because it is a ratio of two lengths. Any complicated function of a dimensionless quantity is also dimensionless, so the only dimensions come from the variable $a,$ a length. This is an expression for the length of a certain spiral, called an "Archimedean spiral".
